segments.title: AJ Savage bombers over Midway
segments.question: How would 50 time-traveling AJ Savage bombers perform if they arrived at the Battle of Midway?
segments.answerShort: They would bring overwhelming bomb load and speed, but carrier recovery would not be realistic, so Pearl Harbor airfields become the practical base.
segments.summary: A Midway counterfactual uses the AJ Savage to show how payload, basing, and carrier recovery limits shape naval air power.
segments.body: The answer treats the AJ Savage as a powerful but awkward intervention in a carrier battle. Dr. Clarke stresses that the aircraft could carry an enormous conventional or nuclear bomb load for the period, yet the nearby US carriers would not be suitable recovery platforms, making Pearl Harbor the plausible operating base.

evidence.note: The question names Midway and AJ Savages, and the answer discusses fleet numbers, carrier recovery, Pearl Harbor basing, and bomb load.

segments.title: Automated loading before Dreadnought
segments.question: How early in the dreadnought era did development of fully automated loading systems start?
segments.answerShort: The answer places early major-gun automated-loading attempts around 1901, before HMS Dreadnought, alongside late-1890s dreadnought-era thinking.
segments.summary: A gunnery question points back before Dreadnought to early experiments with automated major-gun loading.
segments.body: This watch point is useful for separating dreadnought construction from the longer technical conversation around heavy guns. Dr. Clarke dates the first attempts at automated loading for major guns to about 1901 and notes that the ideas later associated with Dreadnought were already being debated before the ship itself.

evidence.note: The answer gives about 1901 for automated major-gun loading and connects dreadnought ideas to late-1890s discussion.

segments.title: British surplus ships for Finland
segments.question: Should Finland have asked Britain for ships headed to the breakers after the First World War?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke says cruisers, torpedo boats, sloops, and possibly monitor or battleship guns could have strengthened Finland, though many hulls would be worn.
segments.summary: A Finnish force-structure answer weighs surplus Royal Navy cruisers, torpedo craft, sloops, and heavy guns against age and usefulness.
segments.body: The answer frames surplus purchase as tempting because Finland had shipbuilding skill but could still benefit from ready-made combatants. Town-class cruisers are presented as especially attractive for modification, while torpedo boats and Flower-class sloops offer narrower utility. Dr. Clarke also notes that battleship or monitor guns might be more valuable than the hulls themselves.

evidence.note: The answer lists Town-class cruisers, torpedo boats, Flower-class sloops, and 14-inch monitor or battleship guns as possible Finnish acquisitions.

segments.title: Destroyer aggression in cruiser command
segments.question: How did the Royal Navy get aggressive destroyer captains to tone it down in cruiser and battleship commands?
segments.answerShort: Age, staff structure, and role expectations mattered; cruiser captains were expected to be more judicious, though some cruiser jobs still wanted aggressive officers.
segments.summary: A Royal Navy command-culture answer contrasts destroyer aggression with the different staff and expectations around cruiser command.
segments.body: The answer explains that destroyer command rewarded applied aggression, while cruiser command came with different staff support and a more judicious operating culture. Dr. Clarke also distinguishes between cruiser jobs: distant-station cruising roles were not the same as fleet cruisers expected to keep up with destroyers and give them fast support.

evidence.note: The answer discusses age, cruiser staff structures, destroyer elite culture, and the difference between cruising roles and fleet cruiser support.

segments.title: An Anglo-German alliance system
segments.question: If Kaiser Wilhelm II had shown more tact, could he have obtained an alliance with Britain, and how would that change Europe?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke sketches an Anglo-German-Austrian-Italian bloc against France and Russia, with possible Japanese involvement and major Mediterranean consequences.
segments.summary: A great-power counterfactual maps how naval policy and alliance trust could reshape Europe, Japan's position, and Mediterranean security.
segments.body: The answer begins with the premise that German naval behavior is central to whether Britain can be brought into the alliance system. It then follows the diplomatic consequences: Britain, Germany, Austria, and Italy put France and Russia on the back foot, and Japan might find a combined British-German alignment attractive for both army and navy reasons. The Mediterranean becomes especially important because Italy, Austria, and the Royal Navy together change the options available to France and Spain.

evidence.note: The answer connects German naval development, an Anglo-German-Austrian-Italian alliance, Japan's possible role, trade trust, and Mediterranean fleet consequences.

segments.title: No-treaty Royal Navy renewal
segments.question: Without naval treaties and with 20 years of construction, would the Royal Navy avoid block obsolescence in its cruiser and battleship forces?
segments.answerShort: He argues that a steady program could replace World War I-era battleships by the early 1930s, assuming roughly four new battleships every two years.
segments.summary: A treaty counterfactual turns block obsolescence into a force-generation problem for a continuously building Royal Navy.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the treaty-free scenario as a question of replacement rhythm rather than a single ship design. Using a notional 24-battleship force and four ships every couple of years, he suggests the Royal Navy could have a modern battle fleet by about 1932-33. The point is that continuous construction changes the age profile of the fleet before a later crisis arrives.

evidence.note: The answer discusses block obsolescence, a 24-battleship target, four ships every two years, and a modern fleet by about 1932-33.

segments.title: Hood survives Denmark Strait
segments.question: If Prince of Wales is destroyed at Denmark Strait and Hood survives, does Hood continue the engagement and how does that change the next two years?
segments.answerShort: Hood likely keeps shadowing or engaging while King George V comes up; losing Prince of Wales could push Lion-class battleships or accelerate carriers depending on how Bismarck is stopped.
segments.summary: A reversed Denmark Strait scenario explores Hood's speed, Prince of Wales' loss, Lion-class pressure, and carrier-strike consequences.
segments.body: The answer treats the loss of a new King George V-class battleship as a signal that could revive pressure for the Lion class. Hood, meanwhile, probably continues the engagement or shadows Bismarck with cruisers and destroyers, using speed despite worn machinery. Dr. Clarke also notes that a carrier strike guided by Hood and escorts could shift emphasis toward carrier acceleration instead.

evidence.note: The answer discusses Prince of Wales' loss, Lion-class pressure, Hood shadowing Bismarck, King George V support, and possible carrier-program effects.

segments.title: Radar gunnery in a no-treaty fleet
segments.question: Without naval treaties and with 20 years of new construction, would Britain invest heavily in radar-guided gunnery?
segments.answerShort: Yes; once gun, armor, machinery, and shell improvements hit limits, better targeting information becomes the logical way to increase battleship lethality.
segments.summary: A no-treaty gunnery answer explains why radar targeting would remain attractive in a continuously developing Royal Navy battle fleet.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes that Britain was already experimenting along these lines in the 1910s and 1920s. In a world of continued battle-fleet investment, the logic is straightforward: when ships cannot simply add more guns, armor, or power, better accuracy and targeting become the route to higher lethality. Radar-guided gunnery therefore fits the same development path as improved shells and fire-control accuracy.

evidence.note: The answer cites 1910s and 1920s experimentation and explains radar targeting as the next way to improve battleship lethality.
